We train models at resolution 256 ×256 for unconditional generation on FFHQ [30], LSUN -Cats, -Churches and -Bedrooms [76] and on class-conditional synthesis on ImageNet (cIN) [11]. Effective Discrete Representations Learning the full hierarchy as described in Eq. (2) and without unnecessary redundancies in the data requires to ﬁrst learn a strong compression model via the objective in Eq. (4). [18] demonstrated how to effectively train such a model and we directly utilize the publicly available pretrained models. For training on LSUN, we ﬁnetune an ImageNet pretrained model for one epoch on each dataset. As the majority of codebook entries remains unused, we shrink the codebook to those entries which are actually used (evaluated on the validation split of ImageNet) and assign a random entry for eventual outliers. This procedure yields an effective, compact representation on which we subsequently train ImageBART. Training Details As described in Sec. 3.3, we use an encoder-decoder structure to model the reverse Markov Chain pt−1 θ (xt−1|xt), t<T , where the encoder is a bidirectional transformer model and decoder is implemented as an AR transformer. As the context for the last scale is pure noise, we employ a decoder-only variant to model pT−1 θ (xT−1|xT). Furthermore, to account for the different complexities of the datasets, we adjust the number of multinomial diffusion steps for each dataset accordingly. For FFHQ we choose a chain of length T = 3, such that the total model consists of (i) the compression stage and (ii)n= 2 transformer models trained in parallel via the objective described in Eq.(7). Similarly, we set n= 3 for each of the LSUN models and n= 5 for the ImageNet model. 6rejection rate for cIN sampling 1.0 0.5 0.25 0.05 FID 21.19 13.12 9.77 7.44 IS 61.6 ±0.8 109.5±2.3 146.2±3.8 273.5±4.1 Text-conditional image synthesis on CC [61] Method FID ↓ IS ↓ CLIP-score ↑ TT [18] 28.86 13.11 ±0.43 0.20±0.03 ImageBART 22.61 15.27 ±0.59 0.23±0.03 Table 2: Quantitative analysis on conditional models. Results For each of these settings, Fig. 2 depicts samples of size 256 ×256 generated with Image- BART and a single pass through the learned Markov Chain, demonstrating that our model is able to produce realistic and coherent samples. This is further conﬁrmed by a quantitative analysis in Tab. 1, where we compare FID scores of competing likelihood-based and score-based methods such as TT [18] and DDPM [26]. Regarding other works on diffusion models such as [26] and [65] operating directly in pixel space, we observe that these approaches perform roughly equivalently well in terms of FID for datasets of low complexity (e.g. LSUN-Bedrooms and-Churches). For more complex datasets (LSUN-Cats, cIN), however, our method outperforms these pixel-based approaches, which can also be seen qualitatively on the right in Tab. 1. See Fig. 20 for a comparison on ImageNet. 4.2 Conditional Markov Chains for Controlled Image Synthesis Being a sequence-to-sequence model, our approach allows for ﬂexible and arbitrary condition- ing by simply preprending tokens, similar to [ 18, 53]. More speciﬁcally, each learned transition pt−1 θ (xt−1|xt,c), t>1 of the Markov chain is then additionally conditioned on a representation c, e.g. a single token in the case of the class-conditional model of Sec. 4.1. Note that the compression model p0 θ remains unchanged. Text-to-Image Synthesis Besides class-conditional modeling on ImageNet, we also learn a text- conditional model on Conceptual Captions (CC) [61, 48]. We obtain cby using the publicly available tokenizer of the CLIP model [ 52], yielding a conditioning sequence of length 77. To model the dataset, we choose T = 5 and thus train n= 4 transformer models independently. For the p0 θ, we directly transfer the compression model from Sec. 4.1, trained on the ImageNet dataset. Fig. 3 visualizes synthetic samples obtained with this model for various “image-cloze” tasks. Our resulting model is able to attend to semantic variations in the conditioning sentence (e.g. a change of weather for imagery of mountains) and renders the corresponding images accordingly. In Tab. 2, we evaluate FID [25] and Inception Scores (IS) [58] to measure the quality of synthesized images, as well as cosine similarity between CLIP [52] embeddings of the text prompts and the synthesized images to measure how well the image reﬂects the text. ImageBART improves all metrics upon [18]. Fig. 21 in the supplement provides corresponding qualitative examples for user-deﬁned text inputs. Resolutions Beyond 256×256 Pixels. Our approach is not restricted to generating images of size 256 ×256 pixels. Although trained on a ﬁxed resolution, we can apply our models in a patch-wise manner, where we use the sliding attention window of [ 18] for each scale t >0. As we now incorporate more and more global context while decoding with the Markov chain (which can be thought of as widening a noisy receptive ﬁeld), ImageBART is able to render consistent images in the megapixel regime. See for example Fig. 4, where we use our text-conditional model to render an 7Figure 4: ImageBART is capable of generating high-resolution images. Here, we condition it on text prompts and interpolate between the two descriptions depicted above the image (see also Sec. 4.2). image of size 300 ×1800 pixel and interpolate between two different text prompts. More examples, especially also for semantically guided synthesis, can be found in Sec. A.2. 4.3 Beyond Conditional Models: Local Editing with Autoregressive Models Recent autoregressive approaches, which use a CNN to learn a discrete representation [71], partially alleviate the issues of pixel-wise autoregressive models by working on larger image patches. However, as we show in Fig. 5, even approaches which use adversarial learning to maximize the amount of context encoded in the discrete representation [18] cannot produce completions of the upper half of an image which are consistent with a given lower half. While our approach also models each transition autoregressively from the top-left to the bottom-right, the ability to attend to global context from the previous scale enables consistent completions of arbitrary order, e.g. right-to-left. To achieve this, we mask the diffusion process as described in Sec. A.3. For a user-speciﬁed mask m(e.g. the upper half of an image as in Fig. 5), this results in a forward-backward process pt−1|t−1,m θ , which, by deﬁnition, leaves the unmasked context intact. The reverse process then denoises the unmasked entries to make them consistent with the given context. Fig. 5 (bottom) visualizes this mixing process, where we use a model with T = 3. The ﬁrst column shows the masked input. To start the process we set all masked entries to random entries. The ﬁrst two columns then show (decoded) samples from the masked reverse processes p2,m θ and p1,m θ , which still display inconsistencies. The remaining columns show the trajectory of the process p1|1,m θ , which demonstrates how the model iteratively adjusts its samples according to the given context until it converges to a globally consistent sample. For illustration, we show the analog trajectory obtained with [18], but because it can only attend to unidirectional context, this trajectory is equivalent to a sequence of independent samples and therefore fails to achieve global consistency. The masked process can be used with arbitrary masks, which enables localized image editing with free, hand-drawn masks as shown in Fig. 6. Note that our model does not need to be trained speciﬁcally for this task, which also avoids generalization problems associated with training on masks [ 78]. Combining this property with the conditional models from Sec. 4.2 allows for especially interesting novel applications, where local image regions are modiﬁed based on user speciﬁed class or text prompts, as shown in Fig. 7. To do so, we perform an experiment for unconditional training on the FFHQ dataset, where we train a Taming Transformers (TT) baseline (corresponding to the case T = 2 within our framework) with 800M parameters and three variants of ImageBART withT = 3 (2x400M), T = 5 (4x200M) and T = 9 (8x100M), respectively. Note that for a fair comparison, all models use the same ﬁrst level for compression, and we ﬁx the number of remaining parameters to 800M and distribute them equally across all scales. All models were trained with the same computational budget and evaluated at the best validation checkpoint. In Tab. 3, we assess both the pure synthesis and the modiﬁcation ability of ImageBART by computing FID scores on samples and modiﬁed images (in the case of upper half completion as in Fig. 5). For both tasks, we use a single pass through the reverse Markov chain. We observe that the modiﬁcation performance increases monotonically with the number of scales, which highlights the improved image manipulation abilities of our approach. For unconditional generation, we observe a similar trend, although FID seems to plateau beyond T = 5. Joint vs. Independent Training While it is possible to optimize Eq. (2) jointly across all scales, we found that training is more robust when training all scales independently. Besides the usual separation of training the compression model p0 θ and the generative model pt≥1 θ , training the latter in parallel over multiple scales avoids the tedious weighting of the loss contribution from different scales; an often encountered problem in other denoising diffusion probabilistic models [26]. Efﬁciency with Less Decoder Layers As we implement the conditional transition probabilities pt−1 θ with an encoder-decoder transformer architecture, we are interested in the effect of altering the ratio of encoder and decoder layers in the model. We perform an experiment on LSUN-Churches, where we analyze the effect of different layer-ratios on synthesis quality (measured by FID) and on decoding speed when ﬁxing the total number of model parameters to 200M. The results in the left part of Fig. 8 conﬁrms that it is indeed possible to reduce the number of decoder layers while maintaining satisfactory FID scores with higher decoding efﬁciency. We identity a favorable trade-off between four and six decoder layers and transfer this setting to our other experiments. Finally, we compare our model in terms of sampling speed with the recent state-of-the-art generative diffusion [26, 65] and AR models [18]. The results are summarized in Fig. 8. While consistently being faster than all pixel-based models due to training in a compressed latent space, the increase in runtime w.r.t. [18] is moderate due to the use of encoder-decoder transformers, i.e., a a decrease in pure decoder layers. If a faster runtime is desired, the speed can be further increased by reducing the number of decoder layers even more, see also the discussion in Sec. A.5. 5 Conclusion We have proposed ImageBART, a hierarchical approach to introduce bidirectional context into autore- gressive transformer models for high-ﬁdelity controllable image synthesis. We invert a multinomial diffusion process by training a Markov chain to gradually incorporate context in a coarse-to-ﬁne manner. Our study shows that this approach (i) introduces a natural hierarchical representation of images, with consecutive levels carrying more information than previous ones. (see also Fig. 9). (ii) It alleviates the unnatural unidirectional ordering of pure autoregressive models for image represen- tation through global context from previous levels of the hierarchy. (iii) It enables global and local manipulation of a given input, a feat previously out-of-reach for ARMs. (iv) We additionally show that our model can be efﬁciently conditioned on various representations, allowing for a large class of conditional image synthesis tasks such as semantically guided generation or text-to-image synthesis. 10A Appendix A.1 Hyperparameters & Implementation Details A.1.1 Compression Models experiment section add. samples effective size ﬁne-tuned from [18] trained from scratch compression rate class-cond. More speciﬁcally, we use the ofﬁcial implementation provided at https://github.com/CompVis/ taming-transformers and ﬁne-tune the publicly available model for experiments on LSUN. For FFHQ, we train such a compression model from scratch. See Tab. 4 for an overview. As some of the codebook entries remain unused after training, we shrink the codebook to its effective size when training a generative model on top of it. For eventual entries not detected during evaluation on the subset, we assign a random entry. A.1.2 Hierarchical Representations via Multinomial Diffusion Tab. 5 lists the conﬁgurations of the multinomial diffusion processes for each experiment described in this work (see also Tab. 4). Note that all representationsxt for T >1 have the same spatial resolution, but since each forward diffusion process gradually removes information, we obtain a coarse-to-ﬁne hierarchy. On average, level xt will contain ⌊¯αt ·N⌋valid entries, which we denote as the effective sequence length in Tab. 5. Thus, ImageBART can also be interpreted as a generative compression model as illustrated in Fig. 9: By trading perfect reconstruction quality for compression, one can obtain a signiﬁcantly shorter sequence, still representing a visually plausible image. This provides the basis for learning a generative model that does not waste capacity on redundancies in the data [15] and the compressed space signiﬁcantly lowers the computational demands for training and decoding. A.1.3 Reverse Diffusion with Transformer Models ImageBART is a learned Markov chain, trained to reverse the multinomial diffusion process described in Eq. (5). We can efﬁciently model the conditionals pt θ with a sequence-to-sequence model and follow [72, 42] to implement pt θ with an encoder-decoder architecture. Tab. 6 summarizes the hyperparameters used to implement the conditionals for each experiment. A.2 Details on Conditional Experiments Semantically Guided Synthesis In addition to class- and text-conditional generative modeling, we apply our model to semantically guided synthesis of landscape images [50]. To achieve this we follow [18] and use the discrete representation of an autoencoder model trained on segmentation masks as conditioning cfor our models pt θ. However, since simply prepending chere doubles the total length, which means a fourfold increase in complexity in the attention mechanism, we exploit the fact that the segmentation masks and the images (or their representations) are aligned. More speciﬁcally, within the encoder-decoder architecture, we ﬁrst produce two embeddings e1 and e2 for xt and c, respectively, which are subsequently concatenated channel-wise, thereby keeping the sequence length of xt. With this modiﬁcations, we train a model with T = 5 and individually optimize each scale similar to the unconditional training setting. Here again, we use the compression modelp0 θ pre-trained on ImageNet. For training, we randomly crop the images and semantic maps to size 256 ×256. For testing, however, we again use the sliding window approach of [18] (cf. Sec. 4.2), which enables us to generate high-resolution images of landscapes, as visualized in Fig. 12 and Fig. 13. A.3 Masked Diffusion Processes for Local Editing Previous autoregressive approaches [69] model images directly as a sequence of pixels from the top-left to the bottom-right. Thus, when generating a pixel, only context from neighbors to the left and above can be taken into account. While more recent approaches, which use a CNN to learn a discrete representation that is subsequently modeled autoregressively [71, 18], improve this situation 12Figure 10: Additional samples for class-conditional synthesis results on ImageNet. While our approach also models each transition autoregressively from the top-left to the bottom- right, each transition additionally has access to global context from the previous step. We aim to exploit this fact to obtain novel applications such as consistent completions of upper halfs and, more generally, completions with respect to an arbitrary mask. For any such mask, let mdenote the result of downsampling it to the size of x1 using nearest-neighbor-interpolation, such that mi = 0 gives the positions where context should be used, and mi = 1 gives the positions where new content should be generated. We then deﬁne the masked forward process, qt,m θ (xt|xt−1) = m·qt θ(xt|xt−1) + (1−m) ·δ(xt −xt−1), (10) which only diffuses masked entries, and the masked reverse process, pt−1,m θ (xt−1|xt) = m·pt−1 θ (xt−1|xt) + (1−m) ·δ(xt−1 −xt), (11) which only denoises masked entries. By deﬁnition, running this process forward and then backward again represents the identity on umasked entries such that the given context remains constant. We denote this forward-backward process that starts from a given xt−1 and produces a sample xt−1,m, xt ∼qt,m θ (xt|xt−1), x t−1,m ∼pt−1,m θ (xt−1,m|xt) (12) by pt−1|t−1,m θ and use it to sample with spatial conditioning information. Since it always leaves the unmasked context intact, the reverse process denoises the unmasked entries to make them consistent with the given context. Besides Fig. 5, 6 additional visualizations of this process can be found in Fig. 14. The top shows masked inputs (left), ﬁnal results of upper completions obtained by [ 18] (middle) and by p1|1,m θ (right). The bottom visualizes the trajectory of the masked process, showing the masked input (leftmost column), denoised samples from p2,m θ (ﬁrst column) and p1,m θ (second column), and every other sample from the forward-backward model p1|1,m θ . It demonstrates how the model iteratively incorporates global context from the previous scale to converge to a globally consistent sample at the very right. A visualization of the process on the class conditional ImageNet model is shown in Fig. 17. Additional examples for conditional samples from this process, as in Fig. 7, can be found in Fig. 15 and Fig. 16. 18A.4 Limitations and Societal Impacts Training deep generative models consumes a signiﬁcant amount of energy (see also Sec. A.1 regarding the used hardware; the ImageNet model for example was trained for 19 days). With regard to the environment, it is important that we reduce the energy consumption as much as possible. To take a step in this direction, we followed previous works and relied on a strongly compressed, learned representation of images. Because we can ﬁne-tune the corresponding encoder and decoder models from pre-trained ones, the costs for this step are largely amortized and subsequent levels of our hierarchy beneﬁt from a drastically reduced sequence length. Nonetheless, it should be noted that such a strong compression scheme for images does not result in perfect reconstructions. For applications which require very high ﬁdelity, such a level of compression might be unsuitable due to artifacts in the reconstructed images. Additionally, the use of adversarial learning in this stage can potentiate biases of datasets by its mode-seeking behavior. Both of these issues can be lessened with larger sequence lengths at the cost of higher energy requirements. The transformer architecture which is used to model the transitions in our hierarchy is generally considered to be less biased compared to convolutional architectures. However, it also cannot beneﬁt from useful inductive biases and therefore requires a large amount of data and resources to learn all required relationships. In early experiments, we noticed that on small datasets, such as CIFAR- 10 [39], the transformer models overﬁt before they reach good performance. Thus, in its current implementation our approach requires datasets of sufﬁcient size. Future works should evaluate different architectures, regularizations or augmentations to enable its use on small datasets. On the other extreme, we ﬁnd that with large datasets, the main bottleneck is the computational resources that can be spent on the training of the transformer models. On the largest datasets, Conceptual Captions and ImageNet, we ﬁnd that performance still improves after two weeks of training. Thus, consistent with other works on scaling up generative models, we expect that performance of our model will keep increasing with the available resources. To ensure comparability with other approaches, we use standard benchmark datasets for deep generative models, even if some of them are known to contain offensive content [9]. A.5 Sampling Speed Here, we discuss the effects of varying the number of encoder vs. decoder layers in ImageBART on sampling speed as presented in Sec. 4.4. On each diffusion scale, the encoder layers only have to run once whereas the decoder layers have to run ndata_dim times. This results in an approximate complexity of order nscalesC(nencoder_layers + ndata_dimndecoder_layers), where C is the complexity of a single transformer layer. The speedup from such an encoder-decoder transformer over a decoder only transformer with nencoder_layers + ndecoder_layers layers is therefore ( nencoder_layers + ndecoder_layers ) · (nencoder_layers ndata_dim + ndecoder_layers )−1 . llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper introduces ImageBART, a hierarchical autoregressive model that addresses the unidirectional bias and lack of global context in traditional AR models for image synthesis. Its main contributions are: 1) A novel coarse-to-fine generative process that inverts a multinomial diffusion process in a discrete latent space, enabling bidirectional context. 2) A framework that reduces the exposure bias of AR models. 3) The ability to perform local, user-controlled image editing and inpainting with arbitrary masks without mask-specific training. 4) Achieving high-fidelity results in unconditional, class-conditional, and text-to-image synthesis, outperforming prior AR models.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: ImageBART employs a two-stage hierarchical approach. First, an image is compressed into a discrete latent representation using a VQ-GAN-like model. Second, a fixed multinomial diffusion process is applied to this representation to create a hierarchy of progressively coarser (noisier) versions. The generative model is a learned Markov chain that reverses this diffusion process. Each step of the reverse chain is modeled by an encoder-decoder Transformer, where the encoder processes the coarser representation to provide global context, and the decoder autoregressively generates the finer representation by cross-attending to the encoder's output. Sampling begins with noise and iteratively refines it through the learned reverse Markov chain.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The model was evaluated on several datasets: FFHQ, LSUN (Cats, Bedrooms, Churches) for unconditional generation; ImageNet for class-conditional synthesis; and Conceptual Captions (CC) for text-to-image synthesis. Image resolution was primarily 256x256. The initial compression models were based on publicly available VQGANs, fine-tuned or trained from scratch. The main generative models were composed of multiple Transformer blocks trained in parallel for each diffusion step. Performance was measured using Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), Inception Score (IS), and CLIP score for text-to-image alignment. Comparisons were made against baselines like Taming Transformers (TT), DDPM, and StyleGAN2.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The model has several limitations. 1) The strong initial compression can lead to reconstruction artifacts, making it unsuitable for applications requiring perfect fidelity. 2) The adversarial training of the compression model may amplify dataset biases. 3) The Transformer-based architecture requires large datasets and significant computational resources, showing a tendency to overfit on smaller datasets like CIFAR-10. 4) The training process is energy-intensive, with the ImageNet model requiring 19 days on a single A100 GPU.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on several areas. 1) Adapting the model for smaller datasets by exploring different architectures, regularization techniques, or data augmentations to prevent overfitting. 2) Further scaling the model with more data and computational resources, as performance was observed to continuously improve with longer training on large datasets. 3) Investigating alternative compression schemes or trade-offs to reduce reconstruction artifacts while maintaining computational efficiency.

new_method.method: The proposed research, Grouped Autoregressive Scale Prediction (GASP), presents a principled architectural solution to the primary bottleneck of Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models: their inherently sequential, scale-by-scale inference process. Instead of relying on post-hoc sampling accelerators which offer unpredictable, content-dependent speedups, GASP re-engineers the generative process itself for deterministic, controllable parallelism. The core idea is to shift the prediction target from a single scale to a 'group of scales' within each autoregressive step. This is achieved through three key architectural innovations. First, a **Hierarchical Group-Causal Attention Mask** is introduced, modifying the standard causal mask to enforce causality strictly *between* groups while permitting fully parallel, non-causal attention *within* the target group being generated. This enables the simultaneous prediction of all tokens for multiple scales in a single forward pass. Second, to handle the increased complexity of predicting a scale group, the simple additive feature map (`f_hat`) of the original VAR is replaced with a **Group Conditioning Adapter (GCA)**. The GCA is a lightweight cross-attention module, akin to a Perceiver, that distills the entire history of previously generated groups into a concise and powerful set of conditioning tokens. This provides a much richer, globally-aware context for the complex task of multi-scale group prediction, potentially improving long-range coherence in the generated images. Third, the autoregressive inference loop is modified to perform **Group-wise Ancestral Sampling**, iterating over M groups instead of N scales, which guarantees a predictable reduction in the number of sequential generation steps. This research introduces group size as a new, transparent hyperparameter, creating a direct and principled lever to navigate the speed-quality trade-off. We expect this approach to yield a near-linear reduction in inference latency proportional to the average group size, making high-resolution VAR models viable for interactive applications. Furthermore, we hypothesize that the GCA's sophisticated context summarization will lead to improved sample quality and global consistency. This work pioneers a new direction in autoregressive modeling focused on co-designing architectures for structured parallel decoding, moving beyond sequential generation.

experimental_design.experiment_details:
### Experiment: Latency vs. Group Size Benchmark

**1. Objective:**

The primary objective of this experiment is to empirically validate the core hypothesis of the Grouped Autoregressive Scale Prediction (GASP) model: that increasing the group size (`G`) deterministically reduces inference latency. We aim to quantify the relationship between group size and wall-clock time, comparing the measured speedup against the theoretical linear ideal. This benchmark will serve as the primary evidence for GASP's effectiveness in accelerating Visual Autoregressive (VAR) models.

**2. Hypothesis:**

We hypothesize that the inference latency will decrease nearly linearly with the increase in group size `G`. Specifically, we expect the speedup factor, calculated as `Latency(G=1) / Latency(G)`, to be close to `G`. We anticipate a slight sub-linear scaling due to the computational overhead of the Group Conditioning Adapter (GCA) and the non-causal attention within each group, which makes each generative step slightly more expensive than in a baseline VAR model. However, this per-step overhead is expected to be minimal compared to the gains from reducing the total number of sequential steps.

**3. Experimental Setup:**

*   **Model Variants:**
    *   **Baseline (G=1):** A standard VAR model, which is functionally equivalent to a GASP model with a group size of 1. All causality is enforced at the scale/token level.
    *   **GASP Models (G > 1):** A set of GASP models, each trained with a different fixed group size. Suggested group sizes for a comprehensive benchmark are `G = {2, 4, 8, 16, 32}`. To ensure a fair comparison, all model variants will have a comparable number of total parameters and a consistent base architecture (e.g., same number of transformer layers, embedding dimension, etc.).

*   **Hardware:**
    *   The benchmark will be executed on a representative range of modern GPUs to assess performance characteristics across different hardware generations. A recommended set includes:
        *   NVIDIA A100 (for high-end datacenter performance).
        *   NVIDIA RTX 4090 (for high-end consumer-grade performance).

*   **Software Environment:**
    *   **Framework:** PyTorch 2.0+ with CUDA 12.x.
    *   **Precision:** Mixed-precision inference (`torch.float16` or `torch.bfloat16`) will be enabled using `torch.cuda.amp.autocast` as this is standard practice for optimizing transformer inference.
    *   **Libraries:** `pandas` for data aggregation, `seaborn` and `matplotlib` for visualization, `tqdm` for progress tracking.

*   **Generation Task:**
    *   Unconditional image generation of a fixed resolution, e.g., 256x256 pixels.
    *   The total number of autoregressive tokens (scales) is fixed. For a 256x256 image and a VQ patch size of 16x16, the total number of scales is `(256/16) * (256/16) = 256`.
    *   The number of sequential generation steps will be `256 / G`.

**4. Methodology:**

To ensure reliable and accurate measurements, the following protocol will be strictly followed for each model variant on each GPU:

1.  **Isolation:** The benchmark will be run for a single image at a time (batch size = 1) to measure the latency most relevant to interactive applications.
2.  **Warm-up:** Before timing, 20 warm-up generation runs will be performed. This pre-compiles CUDA kernels and warms up the GPU, ensuring that measurements are not skewed by initial setup overhead.
3.  **Synchronization:** GPU operations are asynchronous. To get accurate timings, `torch.cuda.synchronize()` will be called immediately before and after the timed generation loop.
4.  **Measurement:** The wall-clock time will be measured using `time.perf_counter()` spanning the entire autoregressive generation loop for a single image.
5.  **Repetition and Aggregation:** For each configuration (`G`), the generation process will be repeated for 100 independent runs. The latency for each run will be recorded. This collection of data points allows for robust statistical analysis, including the calculation of mean, median, standard deviation, and 95% confidence intervals.

**5. Example Implementation Snippet:**

Below is a conceptual Python code snippet illustrating the benchmarking process. The `GASPModel` class is assumed to encapsulate the model architecture and its `generate` method contains the Group-wise Ancestral Sampling loop.

```python
import torch
import time
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from tqdm import tqdm

# Assume GASPModel class is defined and can be loaded.
# class GASPModel(torch.nn.Module):
#     def generate(self, num_tokens, group_size):
#         # ... implementation of group-wise ancestral sampling ...
#         pass

def run_benchmark(model_config, group_size, num_runs=100, warmup_runs=20, num_tokens=256, device='cuda'):
    """Runs a timed benchmark for a given model configuration."""
    model = GASPModel(model_config).to(device).eval()
    latencies = []

    with torch.no_grad(), torch.cuda.amp.autocast(dtype=torch.bfloat16):
        # Warm-up phase
        for _ in range(warmup_runs):
            _ = model.generate(num_tokens=num_tokens, group_size=group_size)
        
        # Measurement phase
        for _ in tqdm(range(num_runs), desc=f'Benchmarking G={group_size}'):
            torch.cuda.synchronize(device)
            start_time = time.perf_counter()

            _ = model.generate(num_tokens=num_tokens, group_size=group_size)
            
            torch.cuda.synchronize(device)
            end_time = time.perf_counter()
            latencies.append(end_time - start_time)

    return latencies

# --- Main script execution ---
group_sizes_to_test = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32]
results = []

for g_size in group_sizes_to_test:
    # In a real experiment, load the specific model trained for this group size
    # model_config = load_config_for_group(g_size)
    model_config = {'group_size': g_size} # Placeholder
    
    run_latencies = run_benchmark(model_config, g_size)
    for lat in run_latencies:
        results.append({'group_size': g_size, 'latency_sec': lat})

# --- Analysis and Visualization ---
df = pd.DataFrame(results)

# 1. Plot Latency vs. Group Size
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=df, x='group_size', y='latency_sec', marker='o', errorbar='sd')
plt.xscale('log', base=2)
plt.xlabel('Group Size (G)')
plt.ylabel('Mean Latency per Image (seconds)')
plt.title('Inference Latency vs. Group Size')
plt.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--')
plt.xticks(group_sizes_to_test, labels=group_sizes_to_test)
plt.savefig('latency_vs_group_size.png')

# 2. Plot Speedup vs. Group Size
mean_latencies = df.groupby('group_size')['latency_sec'].mean().reset_index()
baseline_latency = mean_latencies[mean_latencies['group_size'] == 1]['latency_sec'].iloc[0]
mean_latencies['speedup'] = baseline_latency / mean_latencies['latency_sec']

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
sns.lineplot(data=mean_latencies, x='group_size', y='speedup', marker='o', label='Measured Speedup')
plt.plot(group_sizes_to_test, group_sizes_to_test, 'r--', label='Theoretical Linear Speedup')
plt.xscale('log', base=2)
plt.xlabel('Group Size (G)')
plt.ylabel('Speedup Factor (relative to G=1)')
plt.title('Inference Speedup vs. Group Size')
plt.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--')
plt.xticks(group_sizes_to_test, labels=group_sizes_to_test)
plt.legend()
plt.savefig('speedup_vs_group_size.png')
```

**6. Expected Outcome and Analysis:**

The primary output will be two plots:

1.  **Latency vs. Group Size:** This plot will visualize the absolute latency in seconds as a function of the group size `G`. The x-axis will be on a log2 scale to clearly show the impact of doubling the group size. We expect a steep, downward-sloping curve, indicating a strong negative correlation between group size and latency.
2.  **Speedup vs. Group Size:** This plot will show the speedup factor relative to the `G=1` baseline. It will include a `y=x` line representing the theoretical ideal (linear speedup). The measured speedup curve is expected to closely follow this ideal line but fall slightly below it, especially for larger group sizes. 

The analysis will focus on the gap between the measured speedup and the theoretical line. This gap represents the computational overhead of processing larger groups in parallel within a single autoregressive step. We will report a table summarizing the mean latency, standard deviation, and speedup factor for each group size. The results will be interpreted to conclude on the practical viability of GASP as a method for achieving predictable and substantial inference acceleration in VAR models.
experimental_design.experiment_code:
# Required Python libraries:
# torch>=2.0
# pandas
# seaborn
# matplotlib
# tqdm

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import time
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from tqdm import tqdm
import numpy as np
import os

class GroupConditioningAdapter(nn.Module):
    """A mock GCA module to simulate computational overhead."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=1024):
        super().__init__()
        # Parameters to simulate GCA overhead which scales with group size
        self.query_proj = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(embed_dim, embed_dim // 4))

    def forward(self, history_features, group_size):
        # Simulate increased work for larger groups (e.g., more cross-attention)
        # Overhead scales with group size
        # The dimensions are chosen to create a noticeable but not dominant overhead
        simulated_queries = torch.randn(
            1, group_size * 8, self.query_proj.shape[0],
            device=self.query_proj.device,
            dtype=self.query_proj.dtype
        )
        # Simulate cross-attention like computation
        _ = torch.einsum('b...d,de->b...e', simulated_queries, self.query_proj)
        return history_features

class GASPModel(nn.Module):
    """A mock GASP model to simulate the inference process."""
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=1024, num_layers=16):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed_dim = embed_dim
        self.num_layers = num_layers
        # Mock GCA module
        self.gca = GroupConditioningAdapter(embed_dim)
        # Mock parameters representing the main transformer block's workload
        # This workload is constant for each autoregressive step.
        self.main_workload = nn.ParameterList([
            nn.Parameter(torch.randn(embed_dim, embed_dim)) for _ in range(num_layers)
        ])

    def generate(self, num_tokens, group_size):
        """Simulates the Group-wise Ancestral Sampling loop."""
        if num_tokens % group_size != 0:
            raise ValueError("num_tokens must be divisible by group_size")
        
        num_steps = num_tokens // group_size
        # Initial dummy features
        history_features = torch.randn(1, 1, self.embed_dim, device=self.main_workload[0].device, dtype=self.main_workload[0].dtype)

        for _ in range(num_steps):
            # 1. Simulate Group Conditioning Adapter (GCA) overhead
            # This part has a cost that depends on the group size
            history_features = self.gca(history_features, group_size)

            # 2. Simulate the main autoregressive transformer block workload
            # This part has a fixed cost per step, regardless of group size
            for layer_param in self.main_workload:
                history_features = history_features @ layer_param

            # 3. Simulate within-group parallel attention
            # This is another source of overhead that scales with G
            # We model this as a self-attention operation on G tokens
            group_tokens = torch.randn(
                1, group_size, self.embed_dim,
                device=history_features.device, dtype=history_features.dtype
            )
            _ = torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention(group_tokens, group_tokens, group_tokens)

        return history_features

def run_benchmark(model, group_size, num_tokens, device, num_runs=100, warmup_runs=20):
    """Runs a timed benchmark for a given model configuration."""
    latencies = []
    dtype = torch.bfloat16 if torch.cuda.is_bf16_supported() else torch.float16

    with torch.no_grad(), torch.cuda.amp.autocast(dtype=dtype):
        # Warm-up phase
        print(f"--- Warming up for G={group_size} ---")
        for _ in range(warmup_runs):
            _ = model.generate(num_tokens=num_tokens, group_size=group_size)
        
        # Measurement phase
        print(f"--- Benchmarking G={group_size} ({num_runs} runs) ---")
        for _ in tqdm(range(num_runs)):
            torch.cuda.synchronize(device)
            start_time = time.perf_counter()

            _ = model.generate(num_tokens=num_tokens, group_size=group_size)
            
            torch.cuda.synchronize(device)
            end_time = time.perf_counter()
            latencies.append(end_time - start_time)

    return latencies

def plot_results(df):
    """Analyzes the benchmark data and generates plots."""
    print("\n--- Analysis and Visualization ---")
    if df.empty:
        print("DataFrame is empty, skipping plotting.")
        return

    # Use a professional style for plots
    sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="viridis")
    group_sizes = sorted(df['group_size'].unique())

    # 1. Plot Latency vs. Group Size
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    ax = sns.lineplot(data=df, x='group_size', y='latency_sec', marker='o', errorbar=('ci', 95), err_style="band")
    ax.set_xscale('log', base=2)
    ax.set_yscale('log', base=10)
    ax.set_xlabel('Group Size (G)', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Mean Latency per Image (seconds, log scale)', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title('Inference Latency vs. Group Size', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xticks(group_sizes)
    ax.set_xticklabels(group_sizes)
    ax.minorticks_off()
    plt.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--')
    
    filename = "inference_latency_vs_group_size.pdf"
    plt.savefig(filename, bbox_inches="tight")
    print(f"Saved plot: {filename}")
    plt.close()

    # 2. Plot Speedup vs. Group Size
    mean_latencies = df.groupby('group_size')['latency_sec'].mean().reset_index()
    if mean_latencies.empty or 1 not in mean_latencies['group_size'].values:
        print("Baseline (G=1) data not found. Cannot calculate speedup.")
        return

    baseline_latency = mean_latencies[mean_latencies['group_size'] == 1]['latency_sec'].iloc[0]
    mean_latencies['speedup'] = baseline_latency / mean_latencies['latency_sec']

    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    ax = sns.lineplot(data=mean_latencies, x='group_size', y='speedup', marker='o', label='Measured Speedup')
    plt.plot(group_sizes, group_sizes, 'r--', label='Theoretical Linear Speedup')
    ax.set_xscale('log', base=2)
    ax.set_xlabel('Group Size (G)', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_ylabel('Speedup Factor (relative to G=1)', fontsize=12)
    ax.set_title('Inference Speedup vs. Group Size', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
    ax.set_xticks(group_sizes)
    ax.set_xticklabels(group_sizes)
    ax.minorticks_off()
    ax.grid(True, which='both', linestyle='--')
    ax.legend()
    
    filename = "inference_speedup_vs_group_size.pdf"
    plt.savefig(filename, bbox_inches="tight")
    print(f"Saved plot: {filename}")
    plt.close()

    # 3. Print summary table
    summary = df.groupby('group_size')['latency_sec'].agg(['mean', 'std', 'median']).reset_index()
    summary = pd.merge(summary, mean_latencies[['group_size', 'speedup']], on='group_size')
    summary.rename(columns={'mean': 'mean_latency_sec', 'std': 'std_latency_sec', 'median': 'median_latency_sec'}, inplace=True)
    print("\nBenchmark Results Summary:")
    print(summary.to_string(index=False))

def main():
    """Main function to run the full experiment."""
    if not torch.cuda.is_available():
        print("CUDA is not available. This benchmark requires a GPU.")
        return

    device = 'cuda'
    print(f"Running on device: {torch.cuda.get_device_name(device)}")

    # Experiment parameters
    group_sizes_to_test = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 32]
    num_tokens = 256  # Simulating a 256x256 image with 16x16 patches
    num_runs = 100
    warmup_runs = 20
    model_embed_dim = 1024
    model_num_layers = 24

    # Create model
    model = GASPModel(embed_dim=model_embed_dim, num_layers=model_num_layers).to(device).eval()
    
    results = []
    for g_size in group_sizes_to_test:
        run_latencies = run_benchmark(model, g_size, num_tokens, device, num_runs, warmup_runs)
        for lat in run_latencies:
            results.append({'group_size': g_size, 'latency_sec': lat})
    
    df = pd.DataFrame(results)
    plot_results(df)


def test_experiment():
    """A quick test function to verify code functionality."""
    print("\n========== Running Quick Test ==========")
    if not torch.cuda.is_available():
        print("CUDA not available. Skipping test.")
        return

    device = 'cuda'
    test_group_sizes = [1, 4]
    test_num_tokens = 64
    test_num_runs = 2
    test_warmup_runs = 1
    test_embed_dim = 128 # Smaller model for faster test
    test_num_layers = 2

    try:
        model = GASPModel(embed_dim=test_embed_dim, num_layers=test_num_layers).to(device).eval()
        test_results = []
        for g_size in test_group_sizes:
            latencies = run_benchmark(
                model, g_size, test_num_tokens, device, 
                num_runs=test_num_runs, warmup_runs=test_warmup_runs
            )
            for lat in latencies:
                test_results.append({'group_size': g_size, 'latency_sec': lat})
        
        df = pd.DataFrame(test_results)
        # Temporarily suppress plot saving for test
        original_savefig = plt.savefig
        plt.savefig = lambda *args, **kwargs: None
        plot_results(df)
        plt.savefig = original_savefig # Restore original function

        print("\nTest completed successfully.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nTest failed with error: {e}")
    print("========================================\n")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Run a quick test to ensure everything is set up correctly
    test_experiment()

    # Run the full benchmark
    print("========== Running Full Benchmark ==========")
    main()
    print("=========================================")
